reviews.why: Same metric type (average weekly sales per restaurant) but DIFFERENT scope. Side A's evidence is The Cheesecake Factory brand ("average sales per restaurant operating week ... $239,679"). Side B is the Flower Child brand, a separate restaurant concept under the same parent CAKE ("Flower Child average weekly sales were $85,200"). The ~$239,679 vs ~$85,200 weekly figures confirm they track two distinct store populations. Object=same, scope=FALSE (brand-level metric for a specific concept vs the flagship brand), so the SAME test fails on scope. Project rule brand=own-driver means each brand keeps its own driver. Merging would re-introduce the over-merge the pipeline guards against. Keep separate.

Token overlap on "mix" and "sales" only — the underlying objects differ. (1) Different object/metric: "bar_sales_mix" tracks the share of sales coming from the bar/alcohol part of the restaurant ("Bar mix is about 23%"); "delivery_sales_mix" tracks the share of sales coming from the delivery channel ("delivery makes up about 9% to 10% of that mix"). One measures a product/area (bar/alcohol), the other measures a fulfillment channel (delivery). (2) Different mechanism: bar mix moves with on-premise alcohol/bar traffic and incident rates; delivery mix moves with off-premise/online ordering demand. These are distinct sales-composition slices that can move independently. Same company (CAKE) and same broad "% of sales" framing is not enough — both must describe the same slice, and they don't. Keep separate.

Token overlap (consumer, spending) only. Evidence shows they fail all three same-tests. OBJECT/SCOPE: consumer_spending = overall, economy-wide consumer demand ("strength of the consumer spending environment," boilerplate "conditions that impact consumer confidence and spending," "economic pressure out there for the consumer"). high_income_consumer_spending is explicitly scoped to the affluent $100K+ cohort ("income of 100 plus K, those jobs are going away... incomes aren't slowing down"; "top end of consumers really does feel pressure"). A narrow income sub-segment is not the same metric as total consumer spending. MECHANISM (decisive): for broad spending, softness is a headwind (less traffic, trade-down hurts). For the high-income cohort, the documented mechanism is the opposite sign — affluent consumers "move down off of the steakhouses and the expensive independents," so CAKE "benefit[s] some from that," plus a more resilient cohort. Merging would collapse a sign-flipping, segment-specific driver into a generic one — the over-merge failure mode. Keep separate.

Token overlap ("tip","credit") is a false signal; the evidence shows two unrelated concepts. A=fica_tip_tax_credit: its quotes are all about the SITCA program and "lose tax credits" — this is the FEDERAL FICA tip tax credit the employer RECEIVES from the IRS for payroll (Social Security/Medicare) taxes paid on employee tips; object = a tax credit (money in), scope = federal tax / IRS SITCA compliance, mechanism = qualifying for an IRS program. B=tip_credit_wage: its quotes are all about "minimum wage rates" and the "tip credit wage rate in certain states" — this is the LABOR-LAW tip credit that lets employers PAY tipped workers a lower cash base wage; object = a wage rate (money out / payroll cost), scope = state/local minimum-wage law, mechanism = wage-law changes raising labor expense. All three tests fail: different object (credit received vs wage paid — opposite cash direction), different scope (federal tax code vs state labor law), different mechanism (tax qualification vs payroll cost). Keep separate; no SAME_AS.

Both mention the same brand (Flower Child, a CAKE-owned restaurant concept), which is why token overlap flagged them — but they are different drivers on all three tests. (1) Same object/metric: FALSE. A = the acquisition/full ownership integration of the Flower Child brand (an M&A/corporate-control event); B = the off-premise sales mix metric (55% takeout/delivery share of sales). One is an ownership event, the other is an operating sales-channel statistic. (2) Same scope: FALSE. A's scope is corporate ownership ("bringing it fully under our umbrella"); B's scope is store-level/channel sales performance. (3) Same mechanism: FALSE. A drives value via consolidation/ownership of a brand; B drives via consumer purchasing behavior (off-premise demand). Shared brand name is not shared meaning. Keep separate.

These share the brand "Flower Child" but track two different objects/mechanisms. (a) flower_child_acquisition = the M&A event of CAKE bringing Flower Child fully under its ownership ("bringing it fully under our umbrella"). (b) flower_child_sales = the ongoing revenue/sales performance metric of the Flower Child concept (dollar sales totals, growth %, sales per operating week). Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS: a corporate acquisition transaction is not the same thing as a recurring sales revenue figure. Test 3 (same mechanism) FAILS: an ownership/control change versus organic sales generation are distinct mechanisms. Same brand is not enough — per the rule "brand=own-driver," and the acquisition driver is its own object. Keep separate.

Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only — they share the BRAND (Flower Child) but measure different things. Side A is about ACQUISITION/integration: "bringing it fully under our umbrella" — a one-time corporate event of buying/absorbing the concept. Side B is SAME-STORE (comparable) SALES: a recurring quarterly demand metric ("comparable sales increased approximately X%"). Fails all three tests: (1) different object/metric — corporate ownership event vs comp-sales growth %; (2) different scope — acquisition is a one-time deal, comp sales is an ongoing per-quarter performance figure; (3) different mechanism — M&A/integration vs organic customer demand at existing stores. Same brand, two distinct drivers. Keep separate.

Both mention the same brand (Flower Child), but they track different things. "flower_child_acquisition" = the corporate action of buying/fully owning the Flower Child business ("bringing it fully under our umbrella"). "flower_child_unit_count" = how many Flower Child restaurants exist and the plan to grow that store count (38 locations, ~700 potential, ~20% annual unit growth). Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS: one is an ownership/M&A event, the other is a store-count growth metric. Test 3 (same mechanism) FAILS: acquiring a company vs. opening more locations are different drivers. Same brand alone is not enough. Note: b's same_as_variants already point to "flower_child_unit_development" (also a count/expansion concept), which fits b, not a. Keep separate.

Different metrics despite shared "unit"/"flower"/"child" tokens. A = average unit volume (AUV): dollar sales per Flower Child location ("$4.4 million average unit volume", "potential average volumes for a fully mature box"). B = unit count: number of Flower Child stores and store-growth ("operate 38 Flower Child locations... potential for approximately 700 domestic locations... 20% average annual unit growth"). Object differs (sales-per-store in $ vs store count as a number), scope differs (per-location revenue vs total footprint), mechanism differs (per-box demand/sales vs expansion/openings). A company can grow unit count while AUV falls or rises independently. "unit" in "unit volume" means per-store; "unit" in "unit count" means a store itself — token overlap is a false signal. Fails all three SAME tests; keep separate.

Both mention "Flower Child" (a CAKE restaurant brand), but they describe two different objects/mechanisms. "flower_child_integration" (2023 quote: "we continue down the integration path") = the operational work of absorbing/integrating the acquired Flower Child brand. "flower_child_rewards_program" (2025 quote: "much more of a traditional rewards program... an app based program that has points") = a customer loyalty points app. Different object (integration effort vs loyalty app), different mechanism (operational absorption vs points-based customer rewards), different scope. Fails criteria 1 and 3. Token overlap only ("child","flower") because they share the brand name, not the driver concept. Keep separate.

Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only reflects the shared brand name (Flower Child), not the same driver. The two evidence quotes describe different objects/metrics: (a) "flower_child_margin" is about the restaurant brand's profit margin reaching mid-teens to be a growth vehicle — a profitability metric for the business unit. (b) "flower_child_rewards_program" is about an app-based customer loyalty/points program — a marketing/retention mechanism. Object differs (margin/profitability vs loyalty program), scope differs (whole-brand financial performance vs a single customer-facing feature), and mechanism differs (cost/pricing efficiency vs points-based engagement). Brand=own-driver does not make these the same driver. None of the three SAME tests pass. Keep separate.
See above — fails all three SAME criteria (object, scope, mechanism); shared "Flower Child" brand is the only overlap.

The token overlap ("flower", "child") only means both refer to the same brand/concept (Flower Child, a fast-casual restaurant chain owned by CAKE). They are NOT the same driver.  Test 1 (same object/metric): FAIL. A measures a loyalty/rewards program ("app based program that has points"). B measures comparable/same-store sales growth (e.g., "comparable sales increased approximately 6%"). These are entirely different metrics — a marketing program vs a revenue-performance metric.  Test 2 (same scope): Different. A is scoped to the rewards app and its mechanics. B is scoped to store-level sales performance across the whole Flower Child concept.  Test 3 (same mechanism): FAIL. A's mechanism = customer loyalty points driving repeat visits. B's mechanism = same-store sales demand/traffic outperforming industry indices. No shared causal mechanism.  Two of three (object/metric and mechanism) clearly fail. Keep separate.

These share tokens (program, rewards) but fail all three SAME tests.  OBJECT/METRIC — different: - "flower_child_rewards_program" = a specific loyalty product/app for the Flower Child brand ("an app based program that has points"). It names a THING (a rewards program belonging to one brand). - "rewards_program_contribution" = a financial METRIC, the margin/sales contribution of loyalty ("about 100 basis points from a contribution perspective ... from loyalty"). It names a NUMBER (the bps impact of loyalty), not the program itself.  SCOPE — different: - Side A is scoped to ONE brand, Flower Child. - Side B refers to "loyalty" contribution at a broader level (the analyst asks about overall loyalty contribution in bps); there is no Flower Child qualifier, and CAKE operates multiple brands (e.g., Cheesecake Factory). The loyalty contribution discussed is not tied to Flower Child specifically.  MECHANISM — different: - A = the existence/structure of an app-based points program (a product description). - B = the quantified P&L effect of loyalty (~100 bps contribution). One is "what the program is," the other is "how much money the program moves." A product and its financial-contribution metric are distinct drivers.  Both quotes are CAKE transcripts but from different calls (2025-07-29 vs 2025-10-28) and discuss conceptually different things. A program description and a contribution-in-basis-points metric are not the same object, scope, or mechanism. Keep separate — no SAME_AS link.

Same object/brand (Flower Child) but DIFFERENT metric and mechanism, so the 3-check fails.  Side A "flower_child_sales" = TOTAL/absolute sales for the Flower Child concept. Evidence is dollar totals and total-growth: "sales increased 11.0% to $127.5 million for fiscal 2023," "$34.5 million for the first quarter," "$32.2 million, up 11% from the prior year." This mixes new + existing locations (total revenue), driven by both new-unit openings AND existing-store performance.  Side B "flower_child_same_store_sales" = COMPARABLE (same-store) sales, a like-for-like growth rate that EXCLUDES new units. Evidence is explicit: "Flower Child comparable sales increased approximately 6%," "comparable sales increased approximately 5% from the first quarter," "Q1 comparable sales... highest of any of our core concepts." All quotes say "comparable" — the defining same-store metric.  Why they are not the same: 1. Same object/metric? FALSE. Total sales (a dollar figure including unit growth) vs comparable/same-store sales (a like-for-like rate excluding new units) are distinct, standard restaurant KPIs that move independently. 2. Same scope? FALSE. Total sales = all Flower Child locations; same-store sales = only locations open long enough to be "comparable." 3. Same mechanism? FALSE. Total sales grows from new openings + traffic + pricing; same-store growth isolates only existing-unit traffic/check, deliberately stripping new-unit effects.  Numbers confirm divergence: e.g. fiscal-2024 Q1 total sales grew 10.2% while same-store comparable grew only ~5% over the same window — different magnitudes because they measure different things. Merging would recreate exactly the kind of over-merge (collapsing a total-revenue line into a comp-sales line) that must be avoided. Keep separate; do not add SAME_AS.

Different objects, not the same metric. Driver A counts units of ONE brand, "Flower Child" (38 locations, ~20% annual growth target). Driver B counts units of the "aggregate Other FRC portfolio" — Fox Restaurant Concepts, the parent that owns many concepts (10-15% growth target). A single brand's count is a child/subset of, not equal to, the multi-brand portfolio count. The differing growth targets (20% vs 10-15%) confirm they track different populations. Same-scope test fails. Token overlap on "count/unit" is superficial. Keep separate.

Different brands, same metric type only. flower_child_unit_count counts Flower Child locations (CAKE evidence: "operate 38 Flower Child locations"); north_italia_restaurant_count counts North Italia restaurants ("North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants"). Flower Child and North Italia are two distinct restaurant concepts both owned by CAKE. The only overlap is the word "count" — both are unit counts, but they count different real-world objects/brands, so Test 1 (same object) and Test 2 (same scope) both FAIL. Merging would wrongly combine two separate brands' store counts. Keep separate.

Token overlap on "gift card" only; the underlying drivers differ on all three tests. Object: A is a one-time write-down of physical gift-card inventory (a card-stock asset), B is customers redeeming/spending their gift-card balances. Scope: A is a balance-sheet asset impairment (cost/expense), B is a revenue/income-statement flow. Mechanism: A is a non-recurring $7.9M write-down, B is ongoing redemption-driven sales recognition. All three SAME conditions fail, so keep them separate.

Both touch "comp" (same-store sales), so the underlying metric family overlaps — but scope and mechanism differ, so two of the three tests fail.  1. Same object/metric: PARTIAL. Both reference comparable-store sales ("comp"). But A is an actual-performance signal; B is a guidance/forecast number (the "1% to 2% range," "implied 2026 number," "high end of our guide"). Performance-realized vs forward-guidance is a different object even within the same family.  2. Same scope: FALSE. A is explicitly INTERNATIONAL ("all those different geographies," "everywhere around the world"). B's quotes are the company-wide / overall comp guide (the Q1 range guide, total-company implied numbers) with no international restriction. Different geographic scope.  3. Same mechanism: FALSE. A's mechanism = international brand resonance driving real comps abroad. B's mechanism = management setting and tracking a comp forecast (guidance issuance and beat/miss vs the guide).  Scope and mechanism both fail (and object only partially matches), so the rule forces DIFFERENT. The token overlap (sales/same/store) is surface-level only; the embeddings flagged the shared "same-store sales" vocabulary, not the actual meaning. Keep them separate.

Token overlap on "marketing" only — the evidence shows two distinct things. (1) Object/metric differ: "marketing_costs" is a P&L expense line (basis-point increases in operating expenses tied to the Cheesecake Rewards program launch). "social_media_marketing" is a competitive-risk/capability item — the lone quote is about competitors expanding social-media use making it "more difficult for us to differentiate our social media messaging," not a spend line. (2) Scope differs: marketing_costs spans rewards-program launch spending across many filings/transcripts (2023-2025); social_media_marketing is a single 10-K risk-factor mention about messaging differentiation. (3) Mechanism differs: marketing_costs = actual dollars spent driving expense variance; social_media_marketing = competitive pressure on brand messaging effectiveness, no cost figure. A rewards-program marketing expense is not the same as a social-media competitive-differentiation risk. Keep separate.

Both measure "menu mix" (the metric: shift in average check from what customers order), but the SCOPE differs. "menu_mix" is CAKE's consolidated/company-wide menu mix across all its restaurant concepts — evidence is dominated by 10-K/10-Q average-check disclosures and total negative menu-mix percentages (e.g., "a 3.0% negative change from menu mix"). "north_italia_menu_mix" is scoped to ONE specific brand/concept, North Italia, with its own segment figure ("The north Italia mix was negative 2%"). Same metric and mechanism, but consolidated company-wide vs single-concept scope are not interchangeable: a SAME_AS merge would collapse a brand-level driver into the parent's blended figure, losing the per-concept granularity. Scope test fails, so verdict is DIFFERENT. Keep separate; north_italia_menu_mix is plausibly a child/optional-link of menu_mix, not an alias.

Same object/metric (net income margin) and same scope (company-wide, CAKE), but the MECHANISM differs. Side A "guidance" is forward-looking management projection: every quote uses "we expect / we anticipate / we would anticipate ... net income margin of approximately X%" for a future fiscal period. Side B "surprise" is a backward-looking outcome-vs-expectation gap: every quote reports a REALIZED result against guidance ("was at the high end of the range", "exceeded the high end of the guidance", "exceeded the 3.5% guidance we provided", "above our expectations"). Guidance = the forecast; surprise = how the actual landed vs that forecast. These are distinct drivers in the predictor framework (a projection vs a beat/miss event), so mechanism fails. Merging would collapse the forward signal with the realized beat/miss signal. Keep separate.

Different object/metric, scope, and mechanism. A = capital expenditures, the dollars spent building new restaurants (investing cash outflow): "Capital expenditures for new restaurants... were $61.6 million and $36.9 million." B = the profit margin a new restaurant earns and how fast it ramps to mature margin (operating profitability): "bring the early stages of the restaurants up faster... The mature margin is one thing." Money spent to open a unit vs. profitability the unit earns are distinct concepts. Only token overlap on "new"/"restaurant" links them. Fails tests 1 and 3. Keep separate.

These are two distinct restaurant metrics that only share tokens (north, italia, average), not meaning. Fails all three SAME tests:  1. SAME OBJECT/METRIC — NO. "Average check" = the dollar amount one customer spends per visit (per-transaction spend). Evidence A: "average check... averaged approximately $34.60 for lunch and approximately $44.40 for dinner." "Average unit volume (AUV)" = total annual sales generated by one restaurant location. Evidence B: "Average sales per mature location... approximately $8.0 million" and "annualized AUVs of $7.7 million." A per-customer figure (~$35-44) versus a per-restaurant annual figure (~$7.7-8.0 million) are different objects, ~200,000x apart in scale.  2. SAME SCOPE — NO. Check = per individual customer/transaction. AUV = per restaurant location for a full year. Different units of measurement entirely.  3. SAME MECHANISM — NO. Check is driven by menu price, items ordered, beverage/dessert attach. AUV is driven by traffic volume x check x operating days, i.e. total location throughput. AUV is a higher-level aggregate that includes check as only one of several inputs.  Note: side B also carries variant "north_italia_unit_volumes," confirming B's identity is unit volume, not check. Merging would re-create an over-broad fusion (the v2-style over-merge failure mode). Keep separate.

Token overlap (north, italia) only — the brand name is shared, not the metric. The two measure different objects/mechanisms. (1) Object/metric: A = "average check," the dollar amount a customer spends per visit ($34.60 lunch / $44.40 dinner). B = "menu mix," the percentage shift in product mix (-2%), a component of same-store-sales change. A dollar-per-customer level vs. a mix-shift percentage are not the same metric. (2) Mechanism: average check is driven by price plus what is ordered combined into a total spend; menu mix isolates only the product-mix effect (separate from traffic and pricing). They are distinct levers. (3) Scope differs too: A is a fiscal-2024 chain-wide annual average from a 10-K; B is a quarterly year-over-year mix change for North Italia from a transcript. All three tests (same object, same scope, same mechanism) fail, so keep them separate.

Different object/metric. (a) average check = average dollars a customer spends per visit (revenue per ticket: ~$34.60 lunch / $44.40 dinner). (b) restaurant-level margin = profitability percentage at the store level (13.3%–15%, also called four-wall/forewall margin). Same brand (North Italia), but they fail all three tests: different object (per-customer spend dollars vs. store profit %), different scope (per-ticket revenue vs. whole-unit P&L), different mechanism (menu pricing/mix per guest vs. revenue minus all store-level costs). Average check is one input that can lift margin, but the two are distinct metrics. Token overlap on "north"/"italia" is just the shared brand name. Keep separate.

Same brand (North Italia) but different metrics. A = Average Unit Volume: average sales dollars per restaurant location (~$8.0M FY2022; AUVs of $7.7M). B = restaurant count: number of company-owned North Italia restaurants (an integer headcount from fiscal.ai KPI). Test 1 fails (dollars-per-unit vs. count of units — different object/metric) and Test 3 fails (revenue intensity per location vs. footprint size — different mechanism). Token overlap on "north,italia" only matched the shared brand, not the measured quantity. Keep separate.

Same brand (North Italia), but different metrics. A = Average Unit Volume: dollar sales per restaurant (~$8.0M, "annualized AUVs of $7.7 million"). B = restaurant-level margin: profitability percentage (13.3%-15%, "250 basis point improvement"). Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS — revenue scale (dollars) vs profit margin (percent). Test 3 (same mechanism) FAILS — top-line sales per unit vs cost/profit efficiency. Token overlap only matched the brand name, not the measured quantity. Keep separate.

These share only the brand name "North Italia" and are two distinct restaurant KPIs. They fail all three SAME tests: (1) Different metric — A = Average Unit Volume, an absolute dollar revenue level per location (~$7.7-8.0M); B = comparable/same-store sales, a percentage YoY change (+8%, -2%). (2) Different scope — A averages revenue across mature/open units; B is YoY change at comparable restaurants only. (3) Different mechanism — A is a dollar-level snapshot per unit; B is a rate-of-change growth metric. A's evidence quotes are all dollar AUV figures; B's are all percentage comparable-sales changes. Keep separate.

Token overlap flagged only the shared brand "North Italia" (a CAKE-owned restaurant chain), not a shared metric. A = menu/sales mix percentage ("the north Italia mix was negative 2%"), a same-store-sales composition component. B = count of physical locations ("North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants", fiscal.ai KPI north_italia_company_owned_restaurants), a unit/footprint integer. Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS: a sales-mix percentage vs a restaurant count are different measured quantities with different units. Test 3 (same mechanism) FAILS: how menu/price mix moves comparable sales vs how many stores exist. Same brand scope, but two different metrics. Keep separate.

Token overlap (italia, north) only — same brand (North Italia), different metric. A = "menu mix" = sales-mix shift across menu items (quote: "north Italia mix was negative 2%"). B = "restaurant-level margin" / four-wall margin = profitability percent (quotes: "margin... improved to 13.3%", "restaurant-level margin... was 15%"). Fails test 1 (different object/metric: a mix-shift percentage vs a profit margin) and test 3 (different mechanism: what customers order vs cost/price-driven profitability). Keep separate.

Only the brand scope (North Italia) is shared; the metrics differ. A = "menu mix" / "the north Italia mix was negative 2%" is a revenue-side sales-mix metric (what customers order, price/mix). B = "North Italia segment margins" is a profitability metric (cost structure / margin %). Test 1 (same object/metric) fails: mix vs margin are different objects. Test 3 (same mechanism) fails: revenue composition vs cost/profitability are different mechanisms. The token overlap (italia, north) only reflects the shared brand name, not a shared driver. Keep separate.

Different object/metric. (a) north_italia_menu_pricing is about menu price increases at North Italia ("another price increase in October", "the pricing you just took"). (b) north_italia_restaurant_count is a unit/location count metric ("North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants", a fiscal.ai KPI). Same brand (North Italia) and same company (CAKE) but the shared tokens are just the brand name. Pricing vs unit count fail all three tests: not the same metric (price level vs store count), not the same scope (per-item menu pricing vs total restaurant footprint), and not the same mechanism (menu repricing vs new-unit openings). Token overlap is only the brand name, not the underlying concept. Keep separate.

Same company (CAKE) and same brand (North Italia), but different object, scope, and mechanism. (A) north_italia_menu_pricing tracks the price increases the chain takes on its menu — the quotes are about "another price increase in October" and "the pricing you just took... were you behind on pricing." The object is the menu price level / pricing action. (B) north_italia_restaurant_level_margin tracks restaurant-level (four-wall) profit margin as a percentage — quotes cite "Forewall margin... improved to 13.3%" and "restaurant-level margin... was 15%, a 250 bps improvement." The object is a profitability ratio (margin %). Object differs: a pricing action/price level vs. a margin ratio. Mechanism differs: setting menu prices vs. the net of revenue minus restaurant operating costs. Menu pricing is one input that can lift margin, but the driver is an upstream cause, not the same metric as the resulting margin. The token overlap (north, italia) only reflects the shared brand, not a shared metric. The (B) side's same_as_variants are all margin synonyms (four_wall_margin, restaurant_margin), confirming (B) is purely about margin and does not encompass pricing. Keep separate.

Token overlap (north, italia) only flags the same brand/segment, not the same driver. The two measure different objects with different mechanisms.  Object/metric: - A (menu_pricing) = the MENU PRICE LEVELS the company sets. Evidence: "we have another price increase in October... for North Italia" and "the pricing you just took... were you behind on pricing there?" This is a pricing-action lever (a decision/input). - B (same_store_sales) = COMPARABLE RESTAURANT SALES, a measured outcome metric (% change vs prior-year/quarter). Evidence: "North Italia comparable sales increased approximately 8%..." across ~20 filings.  Scope: both are North Italia brand-level, so scope alone overlaps. But scope match is not enough.  Mechanism: pricing is one of several INPUTS that can drive comp sales (alongside traffic, mix, new units). They are cause vs. effect, not the same mechanism. A price increase is a lever the company pulls; comparable sales is the result that the lever (plus traffic/mix) feeds into.  Conditions 1 (same object) and 3 (same mechanism) both FAIL — pricing-input vs sales-outcome. Keep separate. Note: B's same_as_variant "north_italia_comparable_sales" is a true synonym of B, but neither is a synonym of A.

Object/metric differs. Side A is "restaurant-level margin" (also called four-wall/forewall margin) — the per-restaurant operating margin measured at the individual location level (quotes: "Forewall margin for the adjusted mature North Italia locations improved to 13.3%", "restaurant-level margin for the adjusted mature North Italia locations was 15%"). Side B is "segment margin" — a reporting-segment-level profitability metric covering the whole North Italia business segment (quote: "the North Italia segment margins"). These are distinct accounting objects: restaurant-level (four-wall) margin excludes corporate/segment overhead like G&A, marketing, and depreciation allocated above the unit, whereas segment margin is the broader segment-level figure. Scope and mechanism also diverge: restaurant-level is per-location (mature units only in the evidence), while segment margin spans the segment. Because the object/metric and scope are not the same, this fails the SAME test — keep separate.

These measure two different things for the same brand (North Italia). (A) tracks restaurant-level / four-wall MARGIN — a profitability percentage (e.g. 13.3%, 15%) driven by menu price increases and cost structure. (B) tracks TRAFFIC — guest/customer count change (e.g. -4%, -6%) and its mix/price components. Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS: margin is a profit ratio; traffic is a volume/customer-count metric — entirely different objects. Test 3 (same mechanism) also FAILS: margin moves via pricing and cost control; traffic moves via demand/guest counts. Only the brand scope (North Italia) overlaps, which is why token overlap (italia, north) flagged them. The same_as_variants confirm the split: A's variants are all margin terms (four_wall_margin, restaurant_margin); B's variant is restaurant_traffic. Keep separate.

Same metric type (restaurant-level / four-wall margin) and same mechanism (sales leverage, pricing, cost management), but the SCOPE differs. Driver "a" is scoped specifically to the North Italia concept — every quote names "North Italia locations" (13.3%, 15%). Driver "b" is the company-wide / Cheesecake Factory concept restaurant-level margin — its quotes reference "The Cheesecake Factory concept," "Cheesecake Factory restaurant level margins" (16.1%, 18.4%, 17.7%), and overall portfolio objectives. CAKE is a multi-concept operator (Cheesecake Factory, North Italia, Flower Child, etc.), and management reports North Italia margin separately from the flagship Cheesecake Factory margin. The numbers diverge materially (North Italia ~13-15% vs Cheesecake Factory ~16-18%), confirming these track different restaurant scopes. Scope test fails → keep separate. Merging would collapse a concept-specific driver into the company-wide one and lose the distinction the company itself maintains.

These are two distinct metrics for the same brand (North Italia, a CAKE/Cheesecake Factory segment). The token overlap (italia, north) only reflects the shared brand, not a shared metric.  Test 1 (same object/metric): FALSE. "north_italia_segment_margin" measures profitability — segment-level operating margin (quote asks about mature-unit margins). "north_italia_traffic" measures guest count/footfall — traffic was negative 4%, then negative 6% (number of customers visiting). Margin (a profit ratio) and traffic (a volume count) are fundamentally different objects.  Test 2 (same scope): Both are scoped to the North Italia brand, so scope overlaps — but this alone does not make them the same.  Test 3 (same mechanism): FALSE. Margin moves with pricing, costs, and unit economics; traffic moves with customer demand/footfall. Different drivers entirely.  Because Test 1 and Test 3 are false, they must stay separate. Note: "north_italia_traffic" already has a legitimate variant "north_italia_restaurant_traffic" (both traffic), which is the correct grouping — segment_margin is not part of that family.

Object/metric differs. "off_premise_promotions" = marketing/promotional activity directed at the off-premise channel (its evidence: "we do the marketing and that type of offer is really off-premise driven... we do that on occasion"). "off_premise_sales" = the sales/revenue VOLUME flowing through the off-premise channel (its evidence is overwhelmingly a sales-mix metric: "off-premise sales totaled 22% of sales," "Sales through the off-premise channel comprised approximately 21% of our restaurant sales"). Same SCOPE (CAKE, off-premise channel) but different OBJECT (a marketing lever vs. a sales-volume outcome) and different MECHANISM (running promotions/awareness campaigns vs. consumer demand for delivery/at-home dining producing channel revenue). Promotions are a cause/input; off-premise sales are the measured outcome. Note "off_premise_sales" already absorbs same_as_variants off_premise_channel_mix/off_premise_sales_mix, which are all the same volume metric — promotions is not in that family. Token overlap (off, premise) drove the suggestion, but meaning is distinct, so keep separate.
